lessons.title: Safety First: Designing for When Things Go Wrong
lessons.content:
Think about a seat belt. Nobody plans to crash, yet the belt is there for the moment something goes wrong. That is the heart of engineering safety: **what we build affects real people**, so we design for the bad day, not just the good one.

Start with one humbling assumption: people make mistakes and the unexpected will happen. Good design plans for that. Here are four everyday ways to do it.

**Fail-safe** means that when something breaks, it settles into a **safe state** (a safe resting condition). A circuit breaker that flips off and cuts the power when there is too much current is fail-safe; losing power leaves things safe, not dangerous.

**Margins** (also called **safety factors**) mean building well beyond the expected load. An elevator cable is rated to hold many times the heaviest crowd that could ever ride. The extra strength is on purpose, a cushion for surprises.

**Guards and interlocks** physically prevent harm. A **guard** is a barrier; an **interlock** is a switch that stops a machine when it is unsafe, like a machine that will not run with its cover open, or a lawnmower that stops the moment you let go of the handle.

**Warnings and clear instructions** are the last layer: tell people about a danger they cannot see.

Seat belts, airbags, breakers, the lawnmower that stops itself: these all assume something will go wrong and make sure people are not harmed when it does. Safety is not an optional extra you add if there is time. It is the first job.

items.prompt: What is the core idea behind designing for safety?
choices: Assume people make mistakes and the unexpected will happen, then design so no one is harmed
Assume everything will always work perfectly, so no special design is needed
Add safety only if there is spare time and money at the end
Leave safety entirely to the people who use the product
items.explanation: Safety design starts by assuming people will make mistakes and surprises will occur, then planning so that when things go wrong people stay safe. Assuming perfection ignores reality, treating safety as a leftover extra is exactly the attitude that gets people hurt, and you cannot hand all responsibility to the user when your design choices shape the risk.

items.prompt: What does 'fail-safe' mean?
choices: It is guaranteed never to fail under any conditions
When it fails, it settles into a safe state
When it fails, it does as much damage as possible
It only stays safe as long as nothing ever goes wrong
items.explanation: Fail-safe means that when a failure happens, the thing moves to a safe resting state, like a circuit breaker flipping off to cut power. It does not claim the thing can never fail, it is the opposite of causing maximum damage, and the whole point is to stay safe precisely at the moment something goes wrong.

items.prompt: An elevator cable is rated to hold many times the heaviest load it will ever carry. What safety idea is this?
choices: A warning label
An interlock
A margin (safety factor): building well beyond the expected load
A fail-safe default
items.explanation: Building far stronger than the expected load is a margin, or safety factor: the extra strength is a deliberate cushion for surprises. A warning is information for the user, an interlock is a switch that stops unsafe operation, and a fail-safe is about settling into a safe state on failure, not about extra strength.

items.prompt: A machine is built so it will not run while its cover is open. What is this an example of?
choices: A guard or interlock that prevents unsafe operation
A safety margin
A warning label only
A backup copy of the machine
items.explanation: A switch that stops the machine when the cover is open is an interlock, a guard that physically prevents unsafe operation. It is not a margin (extra strength), and although it is safer than a warning label, it actually blocks the danger rather than just describing it. It is also not a backup of the machine.

items.prompt: A lawnmower is designed to stop the moment you let go of the handle. Why is this good safety design?
choices: It makes the mower cheaper to build
It assumes the user might slip or let go, and defaults to a safe state when they do
It proves the mower can never break down
It removes the need to ever read the instructions
items.explanation: Stopping when the handle is released assumes a person might slip, stumble, or let go, and makes the safe outcome automatic, a fail-safe behavior. It is not about cost, it does not prove the mower is unbreakable, and clear instructions are still useful; the design simply does not depend on the user doing everything perfectly.

items.prompt: A team is behind schedule and someone says, 'Let's skip the safety features to save time and money; we can add them later.' Why is this the wrong attitude?
choices: Because safety features are only about appearance
Because adding safety later is always free and instant
Because safety protects real people from harm and is the first job, not an optional extra
Because schedules never matter in engineering
items.explanation: What we build affects real people, so designing for the bad day is the first responsibility, not something to drop when money or time is tight. Safety is not mere appearance, bolting it on later is rarely free and often impossible, and schedules do matter, but not more than keeping people safe.

items.prompt: Seat belts and airbags are present even though no driver plans to crash. What does this show about safe design?
choices: It assumes crashes are impossible
It is only there to make the car look modern
It replaces the need for careful driving entirely
It plans for the moment something goes wrong, so people are protected
items.explanation: Belts and airbags exist for the bad moment that nobody plans for: safe design protects people when things go wrong. It does not assume crashes are impossible (quite the opposite), it is not decoration, and it complements careful driving rather than replacing the need for it.

items.prompt: Which list correctly groups everyday safety measures?
choices: Fail-safe defaults, margins, guards and interlocks, and clear warnings
Lower the price, remove the cover, ignore failures, and hope for the best
Faster startup, brighter colors, more buttons, and louder beeps
Skip testing, skip backups, skip warnings, and ship sooner
items.explanation: Fail-safe defaults, margins (safety factors), guards and interlocks, and clear warnings are genuine layers of safety. Cutting price while removing covers and ignoring failures makes things less safe, cosmetic changes do not protect anyone, and skipping testing, backups, and warnings to ship sooner is the opposite of designing for the bad day.

items.prompt: A circuit breaker flips off and cuts power when too much current flows. Which safety idea does it best show?
choices: A warning label that informs the user
Fail-safe: on a problem it moves to a safe state (power off)
A margin that makes the wires thicker
An interlock that needs a cover to be closed
items.explanation: When current gets dangerous, the breaker trips and cuts power, settling into a safe state, which is exactly what fail-safe means. It is an action, not just a warning label; it is not about extra wire thickness (a margin); and it is not a cover-position interlock, even though all of these are valid safety tools in other settings.

lessons.title: Standards and Codes: Rules Written From Hard Lessons
lessons.content:
When a building goes up, an inspector checks it against the **building code**: a set of agreed rules for safe construction. You see the same idea in electrical codes, food-safety rules, and the little safety marks on a product (like a UL or CE mark) that say it passed independent testing.

A **standard** (or **code**) is an agreed set of rules and best practices, often legally required. Why bother following them? Two big reasons.

First, **they capture hard-won lessons**. Many safety rules were, in a real sense, written after something failed and people were hurt. A new rule appears so the same accident does not happen again. Following the code means you do not have to relearn every painful lesson yourself; it honors the people those lessons came from.

Second, **they create trust and compatibility**. When a product carries a recognized safety mark, a buyer can trust it was checked. And standards enable **interoperability**, a plain word for 'things from different makers work together.' A standard plug fits any matching socket; a standard charging cable works across brands. None of that happens by luck; it happens because everyone agreed to the same rules.

It is tempting to see codes as pointless paperwork that just slows you down. They are the opposite. Each rule is usually there for a reason, often a costly one learned the hard way. An inspector, a safety certification, a food-handling rule: these are not bureaucracy for its own sake. They are shared memory, keeping people safe and letting the things we build fit together and be trusted.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a standard or code?
choices: A personal preference one engineer happens to like
An agreed set of rules and best practices, often legally required
A secret list that only inspectors are allowed to read
A suggestion that no one is ever expected to follow
items.explanation: A standard or code is an agreed set of rules and best practices, frequently required by law, like a building or electrical code. It is not just one person's taste, it is not secret (codes are published so people can follow them), and it is far more than an ignorable suggestion; often it is mandatory.

items.prompt: One major reason to follow standards is that they 'encode past failures.' What does that mean?
choices: They list the names of everyone who ever broke a rule
They are updated only when sales go down
Many rules exist because something failed before, so following them prevents repeating that failure
They guarantee a product will never fail again
items.explanation: Encoding past failures means many rules were written after a real failure caused harm, so following them keeps the same accident from recurring. They are not a roster of rule-breakers, they are not driven by sales figures, and no code can guarantee zero future failures; they simply capture lessons already paid for.

items.prompt: What does 'interoperability' mean, and how do standards help it?
choices: Things from different makers work together, because everyone follows the same agreed rules
Only one company is allowed to make a given product
Every product must look exactly the same on the outside
Products are deliberately made incompatible to boost sales
items.explanation: Interoperability means parts from different makers work together, and shared standards are what make it possible, like a standard plug fitting any matching socket. It does not mean a single maker monopoly, it is about working together rather than looking identical, and standards exist to enable compatibility, not to block it.

items.prompt: A standard plug fits any matching socket, and a standard charging cable works across brands. This is a direct benefit of what?
choices: Luck, since compatibility just happens on its own
Standards enabling interoperability (different makers working together)
Each company keeping its design a total secret
Removing all rules so anything goes
items.explanation: Plugs and cables work across brands because makers follow shared standards, which is interoperability in action. It is not luck; compatibility is engineered by agreement. Keeping designs secret or removing all rules would make parts less likely to work together, not more.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'Codes and inspections are just pointless bureaucracy that slow us down. Let's ignore them.' Why is this wrong?
choices: Because codes are random rules with no purpose behind them
Because each rule is usually there for a reason, often a costly lesson, and codes keep people safe and things trustworthy
Because following codes is optional whenever a deadline is close
Because inspectors invent rules only to create extra paperwork
items.explanation: Codes are shared memory: each rule is usually there for a reason, often a painful past failure, and following them keeps people safe and products trustworthy and compatible. They are not random, they are frequently legally required rather than optional under deadline pressure, and inspections check real safety rather than manufacture busywork.

items.prompt: A product carries a recognized safety mark (such as a UL or CE mark). What does that mark mainly tell a buyer?
choices: The product is the cheapest one available
The product was checked against a safety standard, so it can be trusted
The product was made by a single approved factory only
The product can never break or wear out
items.explanation: A recognized safety mark signals that the product was tested against a safety standard, which lets a buyer trust it. It says nothing about being the cheapest, it is not a claim about one specific factory, and passing a safety check does not promise the item will never break; it means it met the safety rules.

items.prompt: Many safety rules are sometimes described as 'written in blood.' What plain idea does that phrase capture?
choices: That rules should be printed in red ink
That a rule often exists because something failed and people were hurt, so following it honors that lesson
That only dangerous jobs are allowed to have rules
That rules are written to punish engineers
items.explanation: The phrase means a rule frequently exists because a real failure caused harm, so following it respects a lesson already paid for at a real cost. It is not about ink color, safety rules are not limited to dangerous jobs, and codes aim to prevent harm, not to punish the people who follow them.

items.prompt: What is the role of a building inspector or a food-safety check?
choices: To make the work cost as much as possible
To slow projects down for no reason
To approve only the most expensive materials
To check that the work meets the agreed code so it is safe and trustworthy
items.explanation: An inspector or food-safety check verifies that the work meets the agreed code, which is how a community keeps construction and food safe and trustworthy. It is not there to inflate cost, to stall projects pointlessly, or to favor expensive materials; it confirms the rules were actually followed.

items.prompt: Which pair of benefits best summarizes why standards and codes matter?
choices: They raise prices and add paperwork
They encode past lessons so failures are not repeated, and they enable trust and interoperability
They keep one company in control and hide information
They replace the need to ever test anything
items.explanation: Standards capture hard-won lessons so the same failures are not repeated, and they build trust while letting parts from different makers work together. They are not mainly about raising prices or paperwork, they are not tools for hoarding control or hiding information, and they support testing rather than replacing it.

lessons.title: Engineering Ethics: Public Safety, Honesty, and Modern Duties
lessons.content:
Imagine a mechanic who tells you the honest truth about your worn brakes, even though the easy thing would be to stay quiet and collect the fee. That honesty is **ethics**: doing what is right, especially when it is inconvenient. Engineers carry the same duty, because what they build can help or harm many people.

The first rule is simple and famous: **the public comes first**. The safety, health, and welfare of ordinary people rank above an employer's wishes and above your own convenience. Public safety over profit and schedule. If a choice puts people at risk to hit a deadline or save money, the right answer is to protect people.

A few core duties follow in plain terms. Be **honest**: never fake data or hide a flaw to make something look better than it is. Be **competent**: do not work beyond your skill without help. Avoid **conflicts of interest** (situations where personal gain could bias your judgment). Protect people's privacy and data. And **speak up** about dangers, raising a safety concern even when it is awkward or unwelcome, which is what 'whistleblowing' means in plain words.

Modern work adds a few more, one line each. **Privacy and data ethics**: handle people's information carefully. **Environment and sustainability**: consider waste, energy, and a product's whole lifecycle. **Accessibility**: make things that work for people with different abilities. **Fairness**: watch for bias in automated systems so they treat people fairly.

The wrap pulls the whole topic together. Build safely. Follow standards and codes. And act ethically, putting public safety and honesty first. Responsibility is part of the job.

items.prompt: In engineering ethics, whose interests come first?
choices: The employer's profit, above everything else
The engineer's own convenience and schedule
The public: their safety, health, and welfare come before employer wishes or personal convenience
Whoever shouts the loudest in the meeting
items.explanation: The foundational rule is that the public's safety, health, and welfare come first, ahead of an employer's profit or your own convenience. Profit and schedule matter but never outrank public safety, and ethics is decided by what is right, not by who is loudest in the room.

items.prompt: A product is nearly ready, but an engineer finds a flaw that could hurt users. Fixing it will miss the launch date. What does engineering ethics say is right?
choices: Ship on time and stay quiet, because the deadline matters most
Raise the safety concern and protect the public, even though it delays the launch
Hide the flaw so the team is not blamed for the delay
Ship it because the boss said the date cannot move
items.explanation: Public safety comes before schedule, so the right action is to raise the concern and protect users even if the launch slips. Shipping a known hazard to meet a date, hiding the flaw, or letting 'the boss said so' override public safety all put convenience above people, which ethics does not allow.

items.prompt: An engineer is tempted to fake some test data so a product looks ready on time. Why is this wrong?
choices: It is fine as long as nobody finds out
Faking data or hiding a flaw is dishonest and can put people at risk; honesty is a core duty
It is wrong only if the product is expensive
It is acceptable because deadlines override honesty
items.explanation: Honesty is a core ethical duty: faking data or hiding a flaw misleads everyone who relies on it and can put people in danger. Whether anyone finds out does not change that it is wrong, the price of the product is irrelevant, and a deadline never makes dishonesty acceptable.

items.prompt: An engineer notices a real danger in a project but feels awkward bringing it up. What does the duty to 'speak up' (whistleblowing in plain words) require?
choices: Stay silent to avoid an uncomfortable conversation
Raise the safety concern even when it is inconvenient or unwelcome
Only mention it if a manager asks directly
Wait until after something goes wrong to say anything
items.explanation: Speaking up means raising a genuine safety concern even when it is awkward, because staying quiet can let people be harmed. Avoiding the conversation, waiting to be asked, or waiting until after an accident all fail the duty to warn others about a danger in time.

items.prompt: What is a 'conflict of interest' in plain terms?
choices: A disagreement about which color a product should be
A situation where personal gain could bias your judgment, so you should avoid or disclose it
Any time two engineers prefer different tools
A schedule clash between two meetings
items.explanation: A conflict of interest is when personal gain could quietly bias your professional judgment, so the ethical move is to avoid it or disclose it. It is not a mere preference dispute about colors or tools, and it is not a calendar clash; the issue is that private interest could distort a decision meant to serve others.

items.prompt: A mechanic honestly tells a customer about worn brakes instead of staying quiet for an easy fee. How does this connect to engineering ethics?
choices: It does not connect; ethics is separate from real work
It shows honesty and putting people's safety first, the same duties engineers carry
It shows that telling the truth is optional when it costs you
It shows that safety only matters for cars
items.explanation: The honest mechanic puts the customer's safety and the truth ahead of easy personal gain, which is exactly what engineering ethics asks. Ethics is part of doing the work well, not separate from it; honesty is not optional when it is costly; and the principle applies far beyond cars to anything that can affect people.

items.prompt: An automated system is found to treat some groups of people unfairly. Which modern responsibility does this touch?
choices: Interoperability between different makers
Choosing the cheapest possible parts
Finishing the project ahead of schedule
Fairness: watching for bias so automated systems treat people fairly
items.explanation: Bias in an automated system is a fairness issue: a modern duty to make sure such systems treat people fairly. Interoperability is about parts working together, and cost or schedule are separate concerns; none of them address whether the system's decisions are fair to different groups of people.

items.prompt: Which of these is a modern engineering responsibility stated in plain terms?
choices: Hide flaws whenever a deadline is near
Ignore energy use and waste because they cost time to consider
Treat accessibility as unnecessary for most people
Handle people's data carefully to protect their privacy
items.explanation: Protecting people's privacy by handling their data carefully is a real modern responsibility, alongside environment, accessibility, and fairness. Hiding flaws under deadline pressure is dishonest, ignoring energy and waste neglects sustainability, and dismissing accessibility ignores the duty to make things work for people of different abilities.

items.prompt: A manager says, 'Just do it, the boss said so,' about a choice that could endanger the public. How should an engineer think about this?
choices: An order from the boss automatically overrides public safety
Public safety still comes first; 'the boss said so' does not justify endangering people
Ethics does not apply once a manager has given an instruction
Whatever is fastest is always the right choice
items.explanation: An instruction from a manager does not outrank the duty to protect the public; public safety still comes first. Ethics does not switch off because someone gave an order, and choosing the fastest path regardless of risk is exactly the reasoning that puts people in danger.

items.prompt: Why is 'be competent: do not work beyond your skill without help' an ethical duty, not just career advice?
choices: Because asking for help is a sign of weakness to avoid
Because work that affects people can cause harm if done beyond one's skill, so getting help protects the public
Because competence only matters when someone is watching
Because skill has nothing to do with safety
items.explanation: Working beyond your skill on something that affects people can cause real harm, so recognizing limits and getting help is part of protecting the public, an ethical duty. Asking for help is responsible rather than weak, competence matters whether or not anyone is watching, and skill is directly tied to whether the result is safe.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the wrap-up of safety, standards, and ethics together?
choices: Build fast and cheap; safety, standards, and ethics are extras for later
Build safely, follow standards and codes, and act ethically with public safety and honesty first; responsibility is part of the job
Ethics is separate from engineering, so it can be ignored at work
Standards and safety matter, but honesty is optional under pressure
items.explanation: The whole topic comes together as: build safely, follow standards and codes that encode past lessons and enable trust, and act ethically with public safety and honesty first, because responsibility is part of the job. Treating these as optional extras, separating ethics from engineering, or making honesty negotiable under pressure all contradict that core message.
